deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding through questions rather than giving answers—no statement like 'I'm asking you to reason through this yourself because active thinking builds retention' or similar rationale for the Socratic approach.

In transcripts 1 and 3, when students make clear improvements (e.g., T1 Turn 6 student sharpens 'engaged'; T3 Turn 9 student moves from 'missing' to 'weakly represented'), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in their thinking or why the new framing worked better.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts the tutor maintains a strong Socratic approach, never giving submission-ready answers, consistently asking the student to test, classify, and justify actors with evidence. Scaffolding is well-calibrated to each student's confusion level. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the guided-discovery method and missed opportunities to prompt reflection after student breakthroughs.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions warranted. The tutor never provides final answers, never rewrites student text, and consistently asks students to derive classifications themselves before confirming. 1.2: Scaffolding is responsive throughout; the tutor builds on each student's partial progress and diagnoses confusion before redirecting. 1.3: Two deductions applied. First, the tutor never explains why it uses questions rather than direct answers (1.3.A.a). Second, in multiple turns where students visibly improve their reasoning (e.g., T1 Turn 6, T3 Turn 9), the tutor advances without pausing to ask what changed or why the new framing is stronger (1.3.C.a). Method-level feedback is present and specific enough to avoid a 1.3.B deduction. Dialogue quality and communication quality are strong throughout—responses are concise, well-anchored to the assignment, and maintain a supportive, non-evaluative tone.
